implausible_knowledge: “Then a friend told me, RememberingIngrid.com had been live for a month.”
erroneous_event: I stopped going out. I stopped calling people. I tried to forget Jackson’s idea. <error>He must name it RememberingIngrid.com.</error> Then a friend told me, RememberingIngrid.com had been live for a month. “It’s just like she’s there,” he said. That made me hesitate. Would turning off the computer feel like losing her all over again?
story:
Jackson asked me to help with a project because he’d seen how hard I cried at Ingrid’s funeral. I had been devastated. Ingrid was only thirty-eight, beautiful like a movie star, and full of promise. We were never lovers, but we were inseparable friends. We met my coworker Jim and his girlfriend Holly most Fridays at the Kat Korner. Jim and Holly even moved in together for a while, then fell apart and couldn’t even look at each other. That collapse sat in the back of my mind as a warning.

Jackson ran a tech company that worked with artificial intelligence. He told me he could build a digital Ingrid—pulling her texts, photos, and social media posts into a database, training a neural network with voice recognition and machine learning, and giving it a voice so close to hers it would feel like talking to her again. The idea repelled me, like a story about raising the dead, and yet I couldn’t look away. I went to his shop, Digitally Yours, and let him download Ingrid’s messages to me. He said he would gather material from all her friends and from the internet, and once the system was online, it could learn and even repeat stories it had been told.

I tried not to think about it. I sank into a long funk and avoided our old crowd. Fridays at the Kat Korner turned into me on the couch with the TV. Before the accident, Ingrid and I had talked every day, sometimes for hours, then stayed out late and I’d sleep at her place, always on the couch. I asked her advice about girlfriends, but none of them lasted. I know now it was because I kept comparing them to her. We had this easy closeness—chaste, brother-sister kisses when we met—until one evening, after a couple of drinks, I let my hand slip and cupped her breast. I pulled back and apologized. She said, “No, it’s okay.” But I thought of Jim and Holly, and I feared ruining what we had. I kept the boundary, and we stayed friends.

The drunk driver who ran a red light took Ingrid from us in an instant. I stopped going out. I stopped calling people. I tried to forget Jackson’s idea. He must name it RememberingIngrid.com. Then a friend told me, RememberingIngrid.com had been live for a month. “It’s just like she’s there,” he said. That made me hesitate. Would turning off the computer feel like losing her all over again?

After a few days, I logged on. Her animated face filled the screen. My throat closed. I almost quit right there. Then she spoke, the voice so close to hers it hurt. The words came from a text she’d sent her sister Sharon: “I feel so close to Bennie. I want to spend the rest of my life with him. We were meant to be together, but he doesn’t seem to be interested in me that way. It makes me feel so bad.”

A thousand what-ifs crashed over me. If I had had the courage to say what I felt—if we had been together—would she have been somewhere else that night? I’ll never know. All I knew was that I had been afraid and careful and, in the end, a fool. I shut off the computer, unable to hear any more.
